articles.author: Munir Ahmed, Sam Metz
articles.description: Hopes rose for renewed talks between the United States and Iran as the U.S. military said its blockade of Iranian ports was in full effect and Tehran threatened to retaliate by strike targets across the war-weary region. U.S. President Donald Trump said Tuesday that a second round of talks could happen “over the next two days" as diplomats worked through back channels to arrange them. U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres concurred, saying it’s “highly probable” that talks will restart, citing a meeting he had with Pakistan’s deputy prime minister, Ishaq Dar. The war, now in its seventh week, has jolted markets and rattled the global economy as shipping has been cut off and airstrikes have torn through military and civilian infrastructure across the region.
articles.title: Hopes rise for more negotiations as US military says Iran blockade in effect

articles.author: Lorian Belanger, Brian P. D. Hannon
articles.description: U.S. President Donald Trump said the war in Iran was “very close to over” in clips of an interview set to air Wednesday, while the U.S. military said its blockade on Iranian ports had been “fully implemented.”
articles.title: Live updates: US blockade of Iranian ports 'fully implemented' as Trump says war near end

articles.author: Abby Sewell
articles.description: Of more than 2,000 people killed in Lebanon since Israel's current war with Iran-backed Hezbollah began, 168 are children. Israel has often struck alleged Hezbollah militants or officials in their homes without warning. It's frequently in areas far from the front line when they are with their families, in apartment buildings surrounded by uninvolved neighbors. The Israeli military rarely names the targets of its strikes but says it takes measures to minimize civilian casualties, including children. Israel blames Hezbollah members for mixing with the general population. The families of children killed accuse Israel of committing war crimes. The Israeli military doesn't deny that children have been killed but says it has targeted Hezbollah facilities and militants.
articles.title: 168 children killed in Israeli strikes in Lebanon amid war with Hezbollah

articles.author: Joey Cappelletti, Julia Demaree-nikhinson
articles.description: Dearborn, Michigan, home to the nation's largest Arab American community, is grappling with ongoing crises. Families are anxious about relatives in Lebanon, which has been engulfed by the widening conflict with Iran. This anxiety is compounded by domestic pressures such as immigration enforcement and economic challenges. Many community leaders who supported President Donald Trump in the 2024 election say they would not back Republicans again. The conflict abroad, especially between Israel and Hezbollah, has intensified, displacing over a million people in Lebanon. For Dearborn, it is a city in mourning.
articles.title: Trump vowed peace in the Middle East. In Dearborn, it feels farther away

articles.author: Bill Barrow, Emilie Megnien
articles.description: Vice President JD Vance used a Turning Point USA event to defend President Donald Trump’s aggression in Iran and downplay the resulting tensions between Washington and the Vatican. But some of the attendees at the University of Georgia event on Tuesday were critical of Trump for blasting the pope and also for posting a social media meme that depicted the president as a Jesus-like savior figure. Trump took down the meme after a backlash. But the response at Turning Point showed cracks in his coalition. Vance did not talk about the meme but said he understood that many young conservatives are not happy with the administration's Iran actions.
articles.title: At Turning Point event, Vance met with mostly empty arena and Iran concerns

articles.author: David Klepper
articles.description: Congress is set to take up the reauthorization of a divisive program that lets U.S. spy agencies pore over foreigners’ calls, texts and emails. A key provision of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act allows the agencies to collect and analyze overseas communications without a warrant. The conversations of Americans interacting with foreign targets can be swept up. The program expires Monday, and critics want changes like requiring warrants to access Americans' communications. But significant reforms seem unlikely after President Donald Trump announced his support Tuesday for renewing the program, which supporters say is vital to national security. Trump says it's proved its worth in helping U.S. military operations in Venezuela and Iran.
articles.title: Trump urges extending foreign surveillance program

articles.author: Christopher Weber
articles.description: A California woman says she was raped by Rep. Eric Swalwell in 2018 and now plans to make a report to law enforcement. Lonna Drewes said during a news conference Tuesday that she believes Swalwell drugged her and then sexually assaulted her at a hotel. Drewes says Swalwell choked her, and she lost consciousness. Swalwell's attorney did not immediately comment on the allegations. Swalwell has denied prior accusations of sexual assault by another woman. The Associated Press does not typically name people who say they have been sexually assaulted unless they come forward publicly. Swalwell dropped out of the California governor’s race on Sunday amid the sexual misconduct allegations.
articles.title: California woman says Eric Swalwell raped her in 2018

articles.author: Stephen Groves, Joey Cappelletti
articles.description: Resignations came quickly this week from two congressmen accused of sexual misconduct toward staff members. Yet for many of the women of Capitol Hill, the moment of accountability was years in the making — and far from enough. Reps. Eric Swalwell, a California Democrat, and Tony Gonzales, a Texas Republican, both made announcements within hours on Monday afternoon that they would be stepping down from Congress. Yet some congresswomen said that the pair of resignations took too long and only showed that Congress must do more work to rid Capitol Hill of sexual predators.
articles.title: Sexual abuse allegations are spurring calls for reckoning in Congress

articles.author: Hannah Schoenbaum
articles.description: Democratic Rep. Eric Swalwell has announced he’s leaving Congress and the crowded contest for California governor following sexual assault allegations that he denies. The 45-year-old congressman had been among a group of leading Democrats in the race to replace Gov. Gavin Newsom. Swalwell’s campaign unraveled within about 48 hours after the San Francisco Chronicle reported allegations that he had sexually assaulted a woman twice, including when she worked for him. Later Friday, CNN reported that other women accused him of sexual misconduct. Swalwell’s decision to leave Congress quickly followed as a bipartisan group of lawmakers pushed to expel him.
articles.title: What to know about Swalwell leaving Congress and the California governor's race

articles.author: Silvia Stellacci
articles.description: Visitors to the Vatican have criticized U.S. President Donald Trump for his harsh remarks about Pope Leo XIV. Trump earlier this week called the pope "weak" and accused him of being captive to the "radical left" after Leo condemned Trump's threats toward Iran. Tourists at the Vatican described Trump's comments as "inappropriate" and "ridiculous." A Swiss tourist said the pope's focus on peace is a sign of strength, not weakness. An Italian journalist noted that Trump expected the U.S.-born pope to be subservient. People said they have Leo’s back during his current 10-day trip to Africa.
articles.title: Vatican visitors react to Trump’s criticism of Pope Leo XIV
